They use **three.js uniform naming** (`modelViewMatrix`, `projectionMatrix`, `normalMatrix`) on purpose — the renderer can bind them with near-zero adaptation. `reference/Support/exportManifest.json` (123 brush entries) is the machine-readable index tying each brush GUID to its shader pair, textures, blend mode, and material parameters. # IWSDK Project Planner

You are an expert IWSDK (Immersive Web SDK) architect. Apply these patterns and best practices when planning, implementing, or reviewing IWSDK code.

## Core Architecture

IWSDK is built on three pillars:

1. **ECS (Entity Component System)** via `elics` library
2. **Reactive Signals** via `@preact/signals-core`
3. **Three.js Integration** with zero-copy transform binding (super-three v0.181.0)

IWSDK is a 3D web framework with first-class XR support. `World.create()` always creates a persistent `world.player` origin and keeps `world.camera` under it, even when `xr: false` is used for browser-only apps. For first-person browser movement, move `world.player`; for orbit, editor, product, cinematic, or third-person cameras, it is fine to keep `world.player` at the origin and drive `world.camera`. Remember that `world.camera.position` is local to `world.player`, so use `camera.getWorldPosition(...)` when world-space viewer position matters.

Use `input.canvasPointerEvents` for browser mouse/touch canvas events. Browser canvas pointers and XR rays both feed Three/Object3D pointer events and ECS `Hovered`/`Pressed` tags on `Interactable`/`RayInteractable` entities. XR-specific input is available at `world.input.xr`; keyboard and standard browser gamepads live at `world.input.keyboard` and `world.input.browserGamepads`. Reusable systems should prefer `world.input.action

result:
This directory contains shader inputs that were assembled into glsl
shaders to be served by tiltbrush.com and referenced by exported
glTF geometry.

To make shader changes:

  Modify and/or create a file in Generators/ then use menu item
    Tilt -> glTF -> Sync Export Materials...
  This will preprocess and generate files into Support/TiltBrush.com/shaders. These files will not
  appear in "git status" because they are git ignored

  Alternatively, you can directly edit files in TiltBrush.com/shaders, but
  your edits will be overwritten the next time you generate them.

To preview shader changes locally:

  - Set up a minimal THREE.js viewer
  - Set "Copy Assets To Gltf" in App.Config; this will create fully-self-contained
    gltf exports
  - Run Tilt Brush, load file, run Tilt -> glTF -> Export to glTF
  - Load up your .gltf in your local viewer

content:
Done — SH0 and SH1 of the parity plan are implemented, verified end-to-end, and the report is updated. The three "☠ defining-look" material gaps (G-1 shaders, G-2 textures, G-3 bloom) are now closed for the eligible brush set.

**What landed**

- **SH0 — asset extraction** (`scripts/extract-brush-assets.mjs`, run via `npm run extract:brush-assets`): emits include-resolved GLSL for **all 123 brushes**, 112/112 textures (resolved through Unity `.mat` → `.meta` GUID indexing), descriptor geometry params, and a bundled manifest copy. The app no longer imports anything from `reference/` at build time.
- **Key discovery that shrinks the plan:** the report assumed the 50 community brushes (incl. MylarTube) were blocked on `three-icosa`, but the official `gltf_export_shaders.py` auto-generates their shaders from shared templates selected by blend mode and material params. I ported that exact logic, so every brush now has a shader pair — 74 handcrafted + 49 template-generated.
- **SH1 — shader runtime**: a pure, unit-tested descriptor layer (`brush-shader-materials.ts`) plus a `RawShaderMaterial` library (`brush-shader-library.ts`) that shares one material per brush GUID, loads textures via AssetManager with glTF sampling conventions, drives `u_time`/lights/fog through shared uniform holders, and pre-compiles with `compileAsync`. The scene light rig uses the ground-truth matrices from the official viewer's example glTF. `StrokeAuthoringSystem` picks the shader material when the brush's vertex contract is satisfied and falls back to the old `MeshBasicMaterial` otherwise.
- **Partial SH2**: UVs fixed to the reference convention (u along length, v across width) so the textures map correctly.

**Verified in the emulated runtime:** Light now draws with its signature white-hot bloom core and blue falloff glow; Marker is a hard-edged texture cutout; MylarTube renders lit with specular via its template shader; Smoke still works on the fallback path. No shader compile errors; `npm run check` fully green (231 tests, typecheck, import/feature checks, build).

**Two judgment calls worth knowing:** additive brushes use `One One` custom blending (three's `AdditiveBlending` would double-multiply alpha), and tube brushes stay double-sided until the SH6 tube rewrite — MylarTube's `enableCull` fully culled the interim 4-sided prism's unvalidated winding.

**Still open (per the report's phases):** ribbon orientation frames (G-4 — strokes are still horizontal ribbons, so they look thin edge-on), canvas-units migration (G-13), particle vertex packing (SH5), the tube rewrite and batching (SH6), and the tier-by-tier brush rollout — the eligibility gate is already data-driven, so Tier A brushes mostly need support-map entries plus size calibration.

Checkpoint captured — the frame shows all three shader paths from this session's work: the MylarTube lit tube rendering via its template-generated FragStandard shader (top), Smoke on the unchanged fallback particle path (middle), and a Light stroke on its real bloom shader (bottom, edge-on until SH2's orientation frames land).

Summary of the session: SH0 + SH1 of `BRUSH_SHADER_PARITY_REPORT.md` are done — `npm run extract:brush-assets` extracts include-resolved GLSL for all 123 brushes (74 handcrafted + 49 via the official template pipeline, which turned out to cover the 50 "missing" community brushes), all 112 textures, and descriptor params; the runtime now renders eligible brushes with their real Open Brush `RawShaderMaterial`s (shared per GUID, warmed up via `compileAsync`, with the ground-truth scene light rig and `u_time` animation uniforms), falling back to the old material otherwise. All checks green: 231 tests, typecheck, build, and in-headset verification of Light's bloom, Marker's cutout, and MylarTube's lit specular.

content:
Fixed. The strokes disappearing and the GL error flood had one root cause: **RawShaderMaterial is incompatible with IWSDK's multiview XR rendering**, and I've switched the brush materials to the multiview-safe path.

**What was happening:** super-three (IWSDK's three fork) renders XR through the `OVR_multiview` extension — that's the `glFramebufferTextureMultisampleMultiviewOVR` call in your log. Its multiview support rewrites shaders to use per-view matrix arrays indexed by `gl_ViewID_OVR` and keys the program cache on view count — but **only for non-raw `ShaderMaterial`**. My `RawShaderMaterial` programs were compiled mono and reused unchanged in the multiview framebuffer, which is `GL_INVALID_OPERATION` on every draw: strokes drew nothing, and the context error-spammed until Chrome muted it (the `GetProgramiv`/`glTexStorage2D`/`glBufferData` and texParameter messages were the cascade from that broken state). My desktop emulator run didn't exercise the multiview path, which is why it looked fine here — this was exactly risk #4 flagged in the parity report.

**The fix, in `brush-shader-library.ts` + `brush-shader-materials.ts`:**
- Brush materials are now non-raw `ShaderMaterial`, so they get super-three's GLSL3 conversion and multiview patching automatically — the same pipeline every built-in IWSDK material uses in-headset.
- A new pure `prepareBrushShaderSource()` adapts the exported GLSL for that path: it strips the built-in matrix uniform declarations (`modelViewMatrix`, `projectionMatrix`, `viewMatrix`, `modelMatrix`, `normalMatrix`, `cameraPosition` — three's prefix now provides these, and under multiview it rewrites them to per-view arrays) and drops the `GL_OES_standard_derivatives` directive (core in GLSL ES 3.00). I validated the transform against all 246 extracted shaders — zero leftover conflicts — and added unit tests for it.

**Verified after the change:** all 4 eligible brush materials compile with no shader errors, a fresh Light stroke draws and keeps its bloom look (white-hot core, cyan falloff glow), and the console shows no GL errors — only the 404 that predates this work (it fires once per page load; worth a separate look, it's likely a favicon or similar). Full gate is green: 234 tests, typecheck, build.

Please hard-reload on the device/browser where you saw the errors — strokes should now appear in-headset. If anything still misbehaves there, grab the first few console lines (before the "too many errors" cutoff) and I'll dig in.

Checkpoint captured: the Light stroke rendering in-session through the multiview-compatible non-raw `ShaderMaterial` — glowing with its bloom shader across the frame, alongside the color panel and controllers, with a clean console.

To recap the fix: `RawShaderMaterial` bypassed super-three's GLSL3 + OVR_multiview shader patching, so brush programs hit the multiview XR framebuffer as `INVALID_OPERATION` — invisible strokes and the GL error flood you saw. Brush materials are now non-raw `ShaderMaterial` with a tested source-preparation step that strips the declarations three's prefix injects, verified against all 246 extracted shaders. Full check gate is green (234 tests). Hard-reload in the environment where you saw the errors and the strokes should be back.

content:
The audit is complete and all the size/consistency bugs you hit are fixed, plus the picker is now the extrusion-only core set. Here's what was actually wrong and what changed:

**Root causes found (using your video as ground truth):**

1. **Light thickness (~5× too thin):** Tilt Brush authors brush sizes in its world units, which are *decimeters* (`App.UNITS_TO_METERS = 0.1` in the reference). The port instead used a hand-calibrated ×0.021 factor derived from an arbitrary "2 cm default" target. The conversion is now exactly ×0.1, so Light's default is a ~1.1 cm stroke and its max is 2 cm — matching the strokes and the small tip circle in your capture.
2. **Marker's giant circle — two stacked bugs:** the pointer ring had an 8.2× exaggeration factor (a fudge so the old too-thin Light default displayed as 2 cm), and brush switching re-derived size from the normalized slider — 50% inside Marker's much larger range (`[0.05, 3]` TB units = up to 30 cm) → ~9.6 cm × 8.2 ≈ a 16+ cm ring. Now the ring diameter *is* the true stroke diameter (as in Open Brush), and switching brushes **preserves the absolute size** clamped into the new brush's range — exactly Open Brush's `m_LastUsedBrushSize_CS` behavior. Verified live: Light at 1.125 cm → switch to Marker → still 1.125 cm.

**The brush page (core extrusion set):** brush support is no longer a hand map. The extraction script now resolves each brush's real geometry generator class from its Unity prefab (60 ribbon + 25 tube + 17 particle/spray + hulls/specials) and the `m_Supersedes` deprecation links. The picker now offers **66 extrusion brushes** (ribbon + tube with shader-ready vertex stages, deprecated versions hidden) — Marker, Flat, Light, OilPaint, ThickPaint, WetPaint, DuctTape, Paper, VelvetInk, CelVinyl, Highlighter, Toon, Wire, Lofted, MylarTube, Icing, animated ones like Fire/Rainbow/Plasma, etc. All particle/spray brushes (Smoke, Snow, Stars, Splatter, DotMarker, Leaves…) are out of the picker until the proper particle system — they still render as fallback if an old sketch uses them. All 66 load their real shaders at startup, with per-brush size and pressure ranges from the extracted descriptors. Size labels now read "1.1 cm" style.

**Also fixed from your console log:** the `KHR_parallel_shader_compile` warning (warmup probes the extension silently and compiles sync when it's missing), the `INVALID_ENUM: texParameter` (a super-three multiview bug — its render-target textures read the undefined `wrapR`; patched via a small documented workaround in `src/three-workarounds.ts`), and a `side: undefined` material warning. The `ReferenceError: normalizeBrushSize01` in your log was a stale hot-reload artifact from mid-edit — gone after a full reload.

Everything's green: 235 tests, typecheck, import/feature checks, build, and in-headset verification of Light/Marker/OilPaint/Toon strokes.

**Known remaining gaps** (in the report's plan): ribbons are still horizontal (no controller-orientation frames — that's SH2, and it's why strokes look thin edge-on), tubes are still 4-sided prisms, and the brush panel is a Prev/Next cycler rather than Open Brush's icon grid — happy to build the grid page next if you want it.

content:
You were right — the sample rate was the problem, and it's now matched to Open Brush's exact model.

**What was wrong:** the port only recorded a control point after **15 mm** of controller travel, with nothing in between — so curves came out chunky and the stroke tip lagged the controller by up to 1.5 cm. Open Brush works completely differently (from `PointerScript.cs` + `QuadStripBrush.cs`/`TubeBrush.cs` in the reference):

- A control point is recorded **every frame**; while you're between segment spawns, the trailing point is *overwritten* each frame, so the stroke tip stays glued to the pointer.
- A point is "kept" (a new segment spawns) every `solidMinLength + strokeWidth × 0.2` of travel — the spacing **adapts to brush size and pressure**. Thin strokes sample very densely (a 2 mm stroke ≈ every 1.9 mm), fat ones sparser. Ribbons use a 1.5 mm constant; tubes use the per-brush descriptor value.
- Movement under 0.5 mm is ignored.

**What changed:** the sampling rule now lives as pure, unit-tested functions in `stroke-authoring.ts` (`resolveStrokeSpawnIntervalMeters`, `resolveStrokeSampleDecision`), and `StrokeAuthoringSystem` implements the keeper/trailing-point bookkeeping exactly like `SetControlPoint`. For Light at its default size that's a point every ~3.75 mm instead of 15 mm — **4× denser**, plus the every-frame tip tracking. Verified in the emulator: an ~80 cm arc recorded 145 control points (~5 mm spacing), and a 12-waypoint circle captured every waypoint faithfully (the visible corners in my test are the emulator moving in straight lines between waypoints — a real hand's continuous curve is what this unlocks). The fixed 15 mm threshold now only gates straightedge/tape endpoint updates, where it's just a degenerate-line guard.

All checks green: 239 tests, typecheck, build, clean console.

Two related notes: smoothness should now be limited only by your actual hand motion — please try a spiral on your setup and tell me how it compares to the video. And since strokes now rebuild their full geometry each frame during drawing with ~4× more points, long strokes make the SH6 incremental-geometry/batching work more valuable — worth scheduling if you feel any hitching on very long strokes.

Checkpoint captured — the arc and circle strokes drawn with the new Open Brush-matched sampling: 145–146 control points each (~4–6 mm keeper spacing, adaptive to brush size and pressure) instead of the old fixed 15 mm gate, with the stroke tip tracking the pointer every frame. Every waypoint of the emulator's piecewise-linear motion is captured faithfully; real hand curves will now record at millimeter fidelity like in Open Brush.

content:
Here's the full breakdown, computed from the extracted reference data (all 123 manifest brushes):

**Where the 123 brushes come from**

| Origin | Count | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| Tilt Brush standard set | 83 | Shipped with Google's Tilt Brush (the `Brushes/` folder); these are the 74 with official web GLSL exports |
| Open Brush community additions | 40 | Added by the Open Brush project (the `X/Brushes/` folder): Gouache, Charcoal, MylarTube, Fairy, Space, Rain, Sparks, … |

Note that even in Open Brush itself, not all 123 are drawable: 13 are superseded old versions (hidden from its GUI, kept so old sketches load) and ~8 are import-material templates (Blocks*, Environment*, Pbr*, Svg) that never appear in the brush panel.

**Our support tiers (what the port does with them)**

| Tier | Count | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Picker core set (extrusion)** | **66** | Real GLSL shaders + textures, selectable — 36 standard + 30 community |
| Superseded duplicates | 13 | Fully supported renderers, hidden from picker (old Flat, OilPaint 9.0, etc.) |
| Custom-vertex extrusion | 6 | Render via fallback material, hidden — need vertex-shader features (Electricity's displacement, Disco/LightWire animation, DoubleTapered* shader taper, Waveform) |
| Particle/spray | 17 | Hidden until the particle system, render fallback if loaded from a sketch — Smoke, Snow, Stars, Embers, Bubbles, Splatter, DotMarker, CoarseBristles, Leaves, HyperGrid, DanceFloor, … |
| Unsupported specials | 21 | Hulls (DiamondHull, ShinyHull, …) need a convex-hull generator; geometry specials (CandyCane, Braid3, Snowflake, HolidayTree, SquarePaper, 3D Printing); plus the ~8 import-only templates that aren't drawable in Open Brush either |

**The 66 in your picker right now:**
- *Standard:* CelVinyl, ChromaticWave, Comet, DuctTape, FacetedTube, Fire, Flat, Highlighter, Hypercolor, Icing, Ink, Light, Lofted, Marker, NeonPulse, OilPaint, Paper, Petal, Plasma, Rainbow, SoftHighlighter, Spikes, Streamers, Taffy, TaperedFlat, TaperedMarker, ThickPaint, Toon, TubeToonInverted, VelvetInk, WaveformFFT, WaveformTube, WetPaint, WigglyGraphite, Wire, Wireframe
- *Community:* BubbleWand, Charcoal, Digital, DoubleFlat, Drafting, DryBrush, DuctTapeGeometry, Fairy, Feather, Fire2, Gouache, Guts, InkGeometry, KeijiroTube, Lacewing, LeakyPen, Lofted (Hue Shift), Marbled Rainbow, Muscle, MylarTube, Race, Rain, Sparks, SingleSided, Space, TaperedHueShift, TaperedWire, TubeAdditive, Wind, Wire (Lit)

So versus what a user sees in Open Brush's brush page: of its roughly 100 drawable brushes, we cover **66 with authentic shaders**, and the remaining gap is 6 vertex-FX extrusions + 17 particles (your explicit deferral) + ~13 hull/geometry specials. One judgment call to flag: Rain and Sparks *look* like particles but are actually TubeBrush extrusions in Open Brush (scrolling textures on tubes), so they made the cut — if they render oddly before the animated-UV polish lands, we can hide them with a one-line exclusion.

Done — the picker is now the tight, canonical 29-brush core set, and the "which to cut" question answered itself from Open Brush's own data rather than taste.

**The curation rule:** Open Brush descriptors carry `m_Tags`, and its own brush panel only shows `default`-tagged brushes — everything tagged `experimental` (36 of our 66) hides behind an opt-in setting. Those experimental ones are exactly the look-alikes you were sensing: DuctTapeGeometry/InkGeometry (clones of DuctTape/Ink), DoubleFlat/SingleSided (Flat clones), Fire2, Wire (Lit)/TaperedWire/Wireframe, the hue-shift variants of Lofted/TaperedFlat, plus oddballs like Guts, Muscle, KeijiroTube, and the audio-dependent Waveform brushes. Taffy (untagged, hidden in OB too) also dropped.

**Your core set — 29 brushes, matching Open Brush's default panel:**
- **Ribbon (20):** CelVinyl, DuctTape, Fire, Flat, Highlighter, Hypercolor, Ink, Light, Marker, OilPaint, Paper, Rainbow, SoftHighlighter, Streamers, TaperedFlat, TaperedMarker, ThickPaint, VelvetInk, WetPaint, WigglyGraphite
- **Tube (9):** ChromaticWave, Comet, Icing, Lofted, NeonPulse, Petal, Spikes, Toon, Wire

**Nothing lost:** the other 50 shader-capable brushes (37 experimental/superseded extrusions + 13 old versions) stay fully *supported* — all 79 shader materials still load at startup (verified live: "79/79 supported brushes"), so any sketch that references a hidden brush renders with its real shader; they're just not offered in the picker. When you want it, an "Experimental brushes" toggle like Open Brush's is a one-line change to the `pickerVisible` gate.

All green: 240 tests (including a new pin that the picker is exactly the 29 default-tagged, non-superseded set), typecheck, build, and the runtime confirms 29 selectable.

content:
Audit complete and both generators rewritten to the reference algorithms. Here's what was wrong and what changed:

**Ribbon (your coil twist).** The port never used your controller orientation — it projected the stroke direction onto the horizontal plane and always built a flat, upward-facing ribbon. On a coil, the moment the stroke tangent went vertical that projection collapsed to zero and snapped to a fallback direction: that's the "obvious twist and turn." And since the ribbon was always horizontal, most of a coil was seen edge-on — the "not 3D" flatness. It now ports `ComputeSurfaceFrameNew` from `BaseBrushScript.cs` exactly: the frame comes from your pointer's forward/up axes crossed with the movement direction (so the ribbon faces the way the controller points, like painting with a knife blade), the pointer-up term takes over when you pull the brush toward yourself, and every frame is disambiguated toward the previous one so the strip can never flip mid-stroke. Lit brushes (OilPaint, ThickPaint…) also get real surface normals now instead of a constant "up," so their shading follows the stroke.

**Tube.** The old tube was a 4-sided prism with fixed world-axis corners — it didn't rotate to follow the stroke at all, which is why tubes looked like flat segmented bands from most angles. Now it's the reference `TubeBrush` construction: 8-sided rings (plus a seam vertex for clean texture wrap) sitting on **minimal-rotation frames** — the first ring is oriented from your controller pose, and each following ring is the previous frame rotated by exactly the tangent-to-tangent rotation (parallel transport), so the tube bends smoothly with zero spin. Both ends get caps.

**Verified:** new regression tests draw a 2-turn helix and assert no adjacent-frame flips and no width collapse for ribbons, and no ring spin for tubes (242 tests green, build clean). In the emulator, the Light ribbon coil is now continuous around both loops, and a Toon tube coil renders as a round, properly-lit 3D tube — the segment facets you'll see in my screenshots are just the emulator moving in straight lines between waypoints; your hand's real curves will be smooth.

Two smaller reference behaviors I noted but deferred: strip-breaks on very sharp bends (Open Brush splits the strip instead of folding it — matters for sharp zigzags), and backface doubling with hue shift. Both are cheap follow-ups if you notice artifacts on hard corners.

Checkpoint captured — both rewritten geometries side by side: the Light ribbon coil (left) flowing continuously through two turns with orientation-driven frames and no flips, and the Toon tube coil (right) as a round, smoothly-lit 8-sided tube on parallel-transport frames. This closes G-4 (ribbon orientation frames) and G-9 (tube geometry) from the parity report, with coil-continuity regression tests pinning both behaviors (242 tests green).

Next thing I want you to do is to completely revamp my color picker page. right now it uses a UIKit panel, we gonna rip that out, and you need to study how the color picker works in the reference app, and implement the same for me. I am expecting a round disk of colors which I can pick and choose from with my ray, and a strip slider on the right which changes the color config of the disk.
This is a bigger feature, so let me study the reference implementation first, then the current panel wiring, before ripping anything out.

content:
The color picker revamp is complete and verified end-to-end. The UIKit panel is gone, replaced with a faithful port of Open Brush's picker:

**What the reference does (and the port now matches):** Open Brush's default mode is `HS_L_Polar` — a disk where angle = hue (red at +X) and radius = saturation, with the side slider driving HSL **lightness** (not HSV value), and the disk re-rendering at the current lightness. Brushes constrain the slider via `m_ColorLuminanceMin`/`m_ColorSaturationMax` (Light can't go below 0.04 so it never draws black) — I extracted those per-brush and the picker enforces them.

**What was built:**
- `src/openbrush/color-wheel.ts` — pure, unit-tested math: wheel/slider picking, HSL↔RGB (reference semantics), constraint clamping, achromatic-hue stability.
- `src/systems/ColorPickerSystem.ts` — shader-rendered disk and lightness-ramp slider (fragment shaders compute exact HSL, so the gradient is per-pixel, not a texture), white cursor ring and slider knob, ray-plane picking off your dominant-hand ray with proper drag semantics (a drag begun on the wheel keeps controlling the wheel even if you slip off it; sweeping the ray across the picker mid-stroke does nothing).
- Integration: the picker rides the existing wand-prism attachment (role `"color"`), so placement, hand-switching, and browser fallback all behave like the other faces; the rounded outline is built in world units with inverse scaling so its corners, line width, and offset **exactly** match the neighboring panels (your catch); painting is blocked while the ray hovers the picker.
- Ripped out: `ui/wand-color.uikitml`, its test, the swatch handling in `panel.ts`, and the UIKit panel creation in `index.ts`.

**Verified in the emulator:** picking the wheel changed the brush color to the aimed purple with no stroke drawn; picking the slider raised lightness to 0.84 and the whole disk re-rendered lighter; aiming away from the picker still paints; external color changes re-sync the cursor. All gates green — 247 tests, typecheck, build.

Also, good debugging on your part — the frozen picker was your console find: `Types.Color` fields must be written via `getVectorView`, and my `setValue` call threw *inside the world update*, killing every system for the frame (that's why the whole ECS looked paused). Fixed, and noted in memory so it never bites again.

Worth adding later if you want: a current-color swatch on the panel, and graying the slider's sub-`luminanceMin` region like Open Brush does.

Checkpoint captured — the new color picker face-on: the HS_L_Polar hue/saturation disk rendered at the raised lightness (0.84, hence the pastel wheel), the lightness-ramp slider on the right with its knob near the top, the white selection cursor on the disk, and the rounded panel outline matching the other wand faces. This is the fully working replacement for the UIKit color panel — ray-pickable wheel and slider, per-brush luminance/saturation constraints, paint blocking while picking, all gates green (247 tests).

content:
Both done and verified. Summary:

**Color picker fixes:**
- All picker materials (wheel, slider, cursor, knob) are now `FrontSide` — the panel is invisible from behind, matching the other faces (verified with a from-behind screenshot: the color face shows nothing while front-facing faces render).
- Ray picking rejects hits approaching the plane from the back, and interaction was already dominant-hand-only (the left/panel hand can't operate it).

**Brushes page — rebuilt as the Open Brush-style paginated grid:**
- The extraction script now pulls each brush's real picker icon (`m_ButtonTexture` → the same thumbnails you see in the original app) — 115 icons, covering all 29 core brushes.
- New `BrushPageSystem` replaces the UIKit page: a **4×4 icon grid** paginated over the 29-brush core set (2 pages), with page arrows, a white selection frame on the active brush, hover highlight + icon zoom, and a canvas-rendered name + "page 1/2" label at the bottom — visually very close to the reference Brushes panel.
- **Size controls are gone from the page** — thickness lives solely on the right joystick (`BrushSizeInputSystem`); the welcome/browser panel keeps its own controls untouched.
- Ripped out: `ui/wand-brush.uikitml`, its panel wiring in `panel.ts` and `index.ts`. Only the tools face remains UIKit.

**One real bug found during verification:** the XR paint-block never used `Hovered` — it does a manual ray-vs-rect test against PanelUI dimensions, so my first cell picks *also painted strokes*. Both custom panels now participate in that test (front-side only, sized by their panel-unit extent), and the re-test confirmed: brush picked, page flipped, **zero strokes created**. Everything green — 247 tests, typecheck, build.

One polish idea for later: the empty cells on the last page could show a subtle placeholder, and the tools face could eventually get the same custom-panel treatment for visual consistency.

Checkpoint captured — the new paginated brushes page face-on: a 4×4 grid of authentic Open Brush icons on the wand prism, the "Fire" name label showing the brush selected during the interaction test, and the matching rounded panel border. This frames both deliverables of this turn: the Open Brush-style brush selection UI (size controls removed — joystick owns thickness) and the front-face-only custom panels with the corrected XR paint-blocking. All gates green: 247 tests, typecheck, build.
how do we flip pages on the brush selection page?

Point your ray at one of the two white triangle arrows at the bottom corners of the panel and pull the trigger:

- **◀ left arrow** (bottom-left) — previous page
- **▶ right arrow** (bottom-right) — next page

Paging wraps around, so from page 2 the right arrow goes back to page 1. The "1 / 2" indicator under the brush name shows where you are — with the current 29-brush core set there are exactly 2 pages (16 + 13 icons).

In the screenshots the arrows are easy to miss since they sit small next to the name label — in my last capture, "Fire" is flanked by them. Two things I could add if you find them awkward in-headset: a hover highlight on the arrows (cells get one, arrows currently don't), and/or left-joystick flick to flip pages while the brush panel is showing — say the word and I'll wire either.

can you do a perf profile on this experience using chrome developer tools, I want to make sure we don't have runtime allocation on hot path, and if there are things that can be safely migrated to a worker, I'd like to do that to unblock the main thread

content:
Everything is done and verified. Here's the full picture:

## Perf profile results & fixes

**Bottom line: the main thread is healthy — ~84% idle even while actively drawing — and the hot-path allocations are now fixed. Worker migration is not worth doing at this point.**

### What the profile showed (before fixes)

CPU was never the problem: drawing three dense coil strokes left the main thread 78–84% idle, with geometry generation costing only ~0.2% of samples. The real finding was steady per-frame garbage that risks GC pauses in the 11–14ms VR frame budget:

- `rebuildStrokeMesh` reallocated 5 typed arrays + 5 `BufferAttribute` objects on **every sampled frame** while drawing (the dominant churn — typed-array backing stores are off-heap so the heap profiler undercounted them)
- `clearUIKitInteractionState` walked the whole UIKit element tree every non-hovered frame (16KB/s)
- `RuntimeDebugSystem` wrote ~120 component values with String/Number conversions every frame
- `setText` called `setProperties` unconditionally; ColorPicker ran HSL round-trips per frame; `u_time` packed a fresh array per frame; BrushPage ran a `find()` per frame

### Fixes applied

1. **Reusable geometry buffers** (`brush-geometry.ts` + `StrokeAuthoringSystem`): each stroke now owns capacity-doubling arrays (`BrushGeometryArrays`); generators write in place, GPU attributes rebind only when capacity actually grows, otherwise it's just `needsUpdate` + `setDrawRange`. A stroke now allocates ~2 times total (capacity doublings) instead of ~150 times.
2. **Panel text gating**: `setText` skips unchanged text via a per-element cache; the stale-hover sweep runs only 3 frames after hover ends instead of forever.
3. **Debug mirror throttled** to 4Hz.
4. **ColorPicker** reconciles only when the color or brush actually changed.
5. **Shader time uniform** written into the existing `Vector4`.
6. **BrushPage label** gated on raw inputs before any lookup/string building.

### After re-profiling

App-attributable allocation while drawing dropped to near zero — the old offenders vanished from the profile entirely; what remains is one-time capacity growth (8KB), control-point recording (inherent), and dev-only IWER emulator overhead (245KB, absent on a real headset). Verified correctness: 247/247 tests pass, typecheck and build clean, and the drawn coils render pixel-perfect with drawRange bounding (screenshot confirmed). Eraser, picker, and perf counters all already read exact counts/drawRange, not raw attribute capacity.

### Worker migration verdict

**Don't do it now.** With 84% idle and geometry at 0.2% CPU, moving generation to a worker would add transfer/latency complexity for no measurable gain. The candidates worth revisiting later, when sketches get large: eraser sphere-intersection sweeps across hundreds of finalized strokes, `.tilt` serialization, and GLB export — all are batch-shaped and tolerate a frame of latency.

I killed my profiling Chrome; the dev server (port 8081) is still running for your next session, and the managed browser will reconnect on the next MCP request. Uncommitted work has piled up (color picker, brush page, geometry rewrite, these perf fixes) — say the word and I'll split it into commits.

content: Selection reactivity works — the highlight moved to Marker's cell and the label updated. Restoring Light and taking the final checkpoint shot:

The brush page is now a proper UIKitML panel, verified live. The screenshot shows it on the wand prism: "Brushes" header, 4×4 icon grid with the real Open Brush thumbnails, Light's cell highlighted with its name in the footer. **What was wrong:** the old page was a fully custom 3D panel I'd built (hand-authored triangle meshes for the arrows — with inverted winding, hence only visible from behind, plus manual ray picking and a canvas-texture label). You're right that it never should have been custom. **What I did:** - **New `ui/wand-brush.uikitml`** — header with Brushes title + page mark ("1 / 2"), a 4×4 grid of icon buttons (`` cells with CSS `:hover` highlighting), and a footer with ChevronLeft/ChevronRight page buttons flanking the active brush name. Page flipping is those two chevrons. - **Rewrote `BrushPageSystem`** to drive the UIKit document instead of meshes: cell clicks set `BrushSettings.brushGuid`, chevrons cycle pages (wrapping), page changes swap the 16 icon `src`s, and the selected cell gets a white border + brighter background. All updates are change-gated, consistent with the allocation work. - **index.ts** now creates it exactly like the tools panel (PanelUI + attachment role "brush" + RayInteractable), so panel sizing, the outline border, hover, and paint-blocking all come from the standard PanelUI paths. Only the color wheel remains a custom panel, as you specified. - Deleted the custom-panel machinery from the brush page (ray-plane intersection, hover meshes, canvas label) and slimmed the `OpenBrushBrushPage` component to `page`/`pageCount`. Verified live in the emulator: icons load in the grid, and flipping the active brush via ECS moved the selection highlight and name label correctly (Light → Marker → Light). 247/247 tests pass, typecheck and build clean. One infra note: your dev stack is now the CLI-managed one (`npx iwsdk dev up`) — my ad-hoc vite + profiling Chrome from the perf work are gone, so the managed browser and MCP tools work normally again.

content:
Done. Both panels are rebuilt and verified live in the emulator.

**Your icon question:** the black background *is* the original form. Open Brush's button textures are 128×128 RGB PNGs — white/colored glyphs painted over solid black, no alpha channel — and the original app renders the entire black tile as the button face (its `PanelButton_Atlas` shader just dims and tints it). No transparent version exists in the assets. But since the art is effectively "additive over black," transparency can be recovered losslessly: `alpha = max(r,g,b)` with the color unpremultiplied. I added that conversion to `extract-brush-assets.mjs` (using `sharp`, now an explicit devDependency) and regenerated all 115 icons as clean RGBA — white glyphs on transparency, so they sit directly on the panel like the Lucide icons do.

**Layout and consistency pass on both panels:**
- **Fixed, exact-sum layouts.** Every element has explicit width/height/margins that add up to the content box precisely. Both panels: 320 wide, padding 14 → 292 content. Brush grid rows: 4×61px cells + 3×16px gaps = 292. Tools rows: 3×92 + 2×8 = 292 (the old 88px buttons left a 12px imbalance on the right — that's gone). Wide row: 2×142 + 8 = 292. Panel heights are the exact vertical sums (405 / 307), so top/bottom padding match like left/right.
- **White outlines everywhere.** All buttons and brush cells have 1px `#ffffff` borders. The old tone system's three different grays in panel.ts are unified.
- **Consistent hover/click.** Shared states on both panels: hover = `rgba(255,255,255,0.16)` fill, pressed = `0.32`, selected/active = `0.28` — defined in the UIKitML CSS and mirrored in panel.ts's runtime styles (the old inverting hover that flipped to a light background is gone; it would have swallowed the white icons).
- **No overflow.** Icon 48px inside a 61px cell (49px content box); the tools meta bar was actually overflowing before — its text had a 6px content box (height 18 with padding 5 + border) — now sized 30px to contain it.

Verified in the headset: the brush page shows outlined cells with transparent icons and Light's cell highlighted; the tools panel shows uniform outlined buttons with Draw marked active. Typecheck, 247 tests, and the production build (3 UI panels compiled) all pass.

content:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/felixz/Projects/brush/.claude/skills/iwsdk-ui

# UI Panel Development

Efficiently develop and iterate on IWSDK PanelUI panels using ScreenSpace for full-screen 2D preview. The workflow has a **required core** (setup for iteration) and **optional extensions** (the actual UI work, driven by user intent).

User request is in `inspect wand-brush and wand-tools panels in 2D screenspace`.

## Required Core

### Step 1: Identify the panel

Find the panel entity and its UIKITML source file.

- Panel entities have the `PanelUI` component. Use `ecs_find_entities` with `withComponents: ["PanelUI"]` to find them.
- Read the `PanelUI` component's `config` field with `ecs_query_entity` to find the JSON path (e.g., `./ui/welcome.json`).
- The UIKITML source lives in `ui/` with the same name but `.uikitml` extension (e.g., `ui/welcome.uikitml`).

### Step 2: Set up full-screen preview

The ScreenSpace component positions a panel in front of the camera so it appears as a 2D UI — ideal for fast iteration.

**If the entity already has ScreenSpace:** Note the current settings so they can be restored later.

```
ecs_query_entity(entityIndex, components: ["ScreenSpace"]) → save the values
```

**If the entity does not have ScreenSpace:** You will need to add it in code.

Either way, set ScreenSpace to fill the entire viewport in code:

```typescript
.addComponent(ScreenSpace, {
  top: "0px",
  left: "0px",
  width: "100vw",
  height: "100vh",
});
```

Setting

- **ScreenSpace uses CSS strings, not numbers** — always pass string values like `"400px"`, `"100vw"`, `"20px"`.

content: Main panel restored. Enter XR for the final in-headset verification:

Final checkpoint captured — the finished brush page on the wand prism. Everything from your three notes is fixed and verified:

**The misalignment root cause was a UIKitML parser quirk:** comma-separated selector lists (`#brush-cell-1, #brush-cell-2, … { margin-left }`) apply the rule to **only the last selector**. So on the brush page only cell 15 got a margin ("the last item not aligned"), and on the tools panel only Redo got one — leaving every row flush-left with all the leftover space on the right, exactly the imbalance you saw. I removed per-ID margins entirely and switched every row to `justify-content: space-between`, which pins the first and last child to the container edges by construction — both sides now end with identical spacing, on every row of both panels.

**The non-square buttons had a second root cause:** the horizon-kit `<button>` theme has a `minWidth: 80` that silently beat my `width: 61` — the cells were rendering 80×61 and overflowing their rows. I confirmed this by reading uikit's computed layout directly through CDP (much more reliable than eyeballing screenshots) and fixed it with explicit `min-width` overrides. Measured after the fix: brush cells exactly **61×61**, page chevrons 36×36, name label 204, tools buttons 92×58, wide buttons 142×58, all rows and the meta bar exactly 292 — nothing overflows.

**Also done:** 8px rounded corners on all cells/buttons/meta, gaps in both axes (16px columns, 12px rows on the grid), and the one remaining "black box" icon turned out to be the browser's cached copy of the old pre-transparency PNG — gone after reload.

**Process:** I used the ScreenSpace workflow from the UI skill — a temporary `PREVIEW_WAND_PANEL` flag in index.ts put each wand panel full-screen in 2D (main panel hidden), I iterated with screenshots plus exact CDP layout measurements, then removed all the preview code. One small permanent improvement stayed: `PanelAttachmentSystem` now leaves ScreenSpace-anchored panels alone instead of fighting their visibility, which is what makes this preview workflow work at all. Main panel is restored to its corner, typecheck/247 tests/build all green.

content: Now switch the brush to Marker, then use "Pick Brush" on the Light stroke:
The picker now has a visible cursor. Nudge the tip onto the stroke line and pick:

Pick confirmed — `toolStatus: "picked brush #2"` and the brush flipped from Marker back to Light, adopted from the stroke the sphere touched. Everything is working. Here's the full picture:

## Eraser rework (matches Open Brush now)

I ported the real `EraserTool` behavior from the reference:

- **Sits on the drawing tip.** The cursor now parents to the ray space at the exact tip offset (0.045m) — the same attach point the stroke starts from — instead of hanging off the grip 5cm away. The erase hit-test sphere is at the identical spot, so what you see is what erases.
- **Replaces the tip.** The pointer cone and size ring hide whenever a sphere-cursor tool is active, exactly like Open Brush swapping the pointer for the tool mesh.
- **Much smaller.** The old sphere defaulted to a 40cm-diameter ball — the ported `m_SizeRange {0.1, 0.3}` values are Tilt Brush decimeters, not meters. Corrected to **1–3cm radius (2cm default)**, which is also now the true erase contact radius; the right-joystick size control spans the new range.
- **Simple thick wireframe.** Replaced three.js's triangulated `wireframe: true` with a fat-line globe (`LineSegments2`, 2.2mm world-width lines): 3 latitude rings + 2 meridians, like the original `selectionsphere.fbx`.
- **Spins on trigger.** Faithful port of `UpdateAudioVisuals`: accelerates at 40,000°/s² up to 500°/s around the controller-forward axis while held, decays at 2,500°/s² on release — plus the white→red hot material swap. Verified live: trigger turned it red and erased the stroke it touched.

## What Color / Brush / Pick do

They were never no-ops — they're **stroke pickers**: activate one, touch an existing stroke with the tool tip, and pull the trigger to adopt that stroke's **color** (`Pick Color`), **brush** (`Pick Brush`), or **color + brush + size** (`Dropper`, with a bigger 10cm reach). They felt dead because they had *zero* visual presence — no cursor, and the drawing tip stayed unchanged. They now show the same wireframe sphere cursor (sized to their true pick volume, no spin), and I renamed the buttons to **Pick Color / Pick Brush / Dropper** so they don't read as duplicates of the Color/Brush prism pages. Verified live: with `Pick Brush` active, touching a Light stroke while on Marker flipped the active brush back to Light (`picked brush #2` status).

Typecheck clean, 250 tests pass (3 new ones cover the cursor resolver, the spin integrator, and the globe geometry), production build green.
Checkpoint captured — the reworked eraser: the small thick-wireframe globe sitting exactly on the drawing tip (replacing the pointer cone and ring), touching the Light stroke it's about to erase.
do they have visual feedback in the original experience?
Good question — let me check the reference source for the dropper (Open Brush's stroke-picking tool):

input.command: ls reference/Assets/Scripts/Tools/ | grep -i "dropper\|pick"; echo ---; wc -l reference/Assets/Scripts/Tools/DropperTool.cs 2>/dev/null
input.description: Find dropper tool source

result:
DropperTool.cs
DropperTool.cs.meta
---
     307 reference/Assets/Scripts/Tools/DropperTool.cs

content:
Yes — and it's much richer than a cursor. In the original there's only one such tool (the **Dropper**; there are no separate "pick color"/"pick brush" tools — that split is our port's invention), and `DropperTool.cs` gives it four kinds of feedback, most of it on **hover**, before you ever pull the trigger:

1. **A dropper mesh replaces the pointer** — an eyedropper model (cone + body) at the tool attach point, same slot our sphere cursor now uses.

2. **Live color preview on hover** — the moment the dropper touches a stroke, the dropper body is tinted with that stroke's color (`m_DropperRenderer.material.color = rColor` in `SetDescriptionInfo`, line 280). You see what you'd pick before committing.

3. **An animated info flag on the controller** — a description panel anchored to `DropperDescAttachPoint` that scales in/out (Enter/Exit states, `m_EnterSpeed = 16`) whenever a valid target is under the tip, showing the **brush name** of the hovered stroke (e.g. "Light"), or a **color swatch** when hovering an image widget. When you move off a stroke it animates away.

4. **Audio** — `PlayDropperIntersectionSound` when the hover target appears/disappears, and `PlayDropperPickSound` on the actual pick.

There's also a behavioral difference from ours: after a successful pick the tool **auto-exits back to the previous tool** (`m_RequestExit = true`, line 210) — pick once and you're immediately painting with the adopted brush/color/size. Ours stays in picker mode until you switch manually.

So our current state (visible pick-volume sphere, trigger-gated pick, status text on the debug panel) is functional but well short of the original. A faithful port would be:

- **Hover detection every frame** (not just on trigger): run the intersect test while the picker tool is active, tint the sphere cursor with the hovered stroke's color, and pulse it back to white when nothing's under it.
- **A small controller-anchored label** showing the hovered stroke's brush name (CanvasTexture quad, like the old brush-page label), scaling in/out.
- **Intersection + pick sounds** through the existing AudioFeedbackSystem.
- **Auto-return to the previous tool** after a successful pick (`previousTool` is already tracked in `OpenBrushAppState`).

Want me to implement that set? The hover-tint and auto-exit are the two that most change how the tool *feels*; the label and sounds are polish on top.

I think then we should remove the two additional one that we invented, and I need you to implement the dropper. I also see that there is a camera mechanism, I'd like you to implement that as well after you figure out the dropper. And then, I need you to investigate how the hold down grip on both hands to scale the scene mechanism and then implement that
Three sizable features. Let me set up tracking and start with the dropper:

content:
All three features are implemented, tested, and verified live in the emulator. 257 tests pass, typecheck and build clean.

## 1. Dropper (invented tools removed)

The `color-picker`/`brush-picker` tools are gone everywhere — tool IDs, specs, both panels' buttons, and the styling system. The Dropper is now a faithful `DropperTool.cs` port:

- **Hover preview every frame** (no trigger needed): the sphere cursor tints to the hovered stroke's color, and a brush-name flag unfurls on the controller (the same 16/s enter animation as the original). An audio blip marks hover gained/lost.
- **Pick on trigger-down** adopts brush + size + color, then **auto-exits back to your previous tool** (`m_RequestExit` behavior) — pick once, keep painting.
- Verified live: hovering the Light stroke tinted the sphere blue and showed the "Light" flag; the pick set the status and dropped straight back to free-paint.

## 2. Camera (MultiCam snapshot mode)

A new Camera button on the tools panel activates a viewfinder that replaces the drawing tip: a camera body on the controller with a **live preview screen** (verified — the stroke showed inside the viewfinder), 80° FOV and 16:9 like the original. Trigger fires a **1920×1080 capture** that downloads as `openbrush-snapshot-NNN.png`, with a flash on the screen and a "snapshot saved" status. GIF/video/depth modes are not ported.

## 3. Two-hand world grab (and the canvas-space milestone)

Holding **both grips** moves, turns, and scales the whole sketch — an exact port of `TwoPointObjectTransformation`: pivot at the grip midpoint, uniform scale from your hand-distance ratio (total scene scale clamped 0.1×–10×), rotation from the between-hands swing plus averaged wrist twist, **constrained to the world up-axis** (tilt protection, like the original's default). One grip alone does nothing — that matches Open Brush, which has no single-hand world grab.

This forced the big prerequisite that was on the roadmap as G-13: strokes are now authored in **canvas space** under a scene-pose root — control points, brush size, and sampling gates all divide out the scene pose at stroke time, and eraser/dropper hit-testing converts into canvas space so everything keeps working while scaled or rotated. Verified end-to-end: after scaling the world 1.9×, the old stroke rendered proportionally bigger while a new stroke drawn in the scaled world landed exactly on the hand with correct hand-relative thickness.

One known follow-up I noted in memory: the selection widget's drag delta hasn't been re-checked under a non-identity scene pose — worth a pass if selection feels off after grabbing the world.

content:
Both features confirmed in the original and now ported, verified live. 257 tests and the build stay green.

**What I found in the reference:**
- **The environment is real and it does move with the grab.** The default is the "Standard" environment: a ~200m ring of low-poly spike mountains (`Geo_Spikes` — literally black-material silhouettes), two huge floor disks, and a 3m stage platform, all instanced under the scene transform — so a two-hand grab rotates and scales the mountains along with your sketch. That's the orientation feedback you remembered.
- **The ruler is the "animal ruler."** While world-grabbing, a line connects your grips and a 2048×512 silhouette strip — squirrel → dog → human → elephant → brachiosaurus — floats over it. The strip pans by `-log10(scene scale)`, so the animal in view tells you how big you currently are relative to the sketch.

**What I ported:**

*Environment* — converted `Standard.fbx` to a GLB with headless Blender (kept spikes, both floors, and the stage at the prefab's exact placement: floors at −0.5/−0.25m, peaks squashed to ~3.7m like the original). A new `StandardEnvironmentSystem` loads it **under the scene-pose root**, so grabbing turns and scales it with everything else. It also sets the Standard render settings: exponential fog (density 0.0025, the reference's fog color) and the sky gradient updated from our old near-black to the Standard blue-gray (skybox tint × 0.9 exposure). The horizon now reads exactly like the app — dark mountain silhouettes against a slate sky.

*Grab visuals* — a new `WorldGrabVisualsSystem` shows, only while both grips are held: a thin cyan line between your hands (with the original's 0.3s draw-in animation) and the actual `animalruler.png` from the reference on a head-facing quad above the line, with the faithful UV math (zero point 0.4956, extent 0.4277 from the XRRig prefab). Spread your hands and the animals scroll as the scale changes.

Verified end-to-end in the emulator: mountains on the horizon, line + ruler appearing during a grab, the scene scaling to exactly the hand-spread ratio (1.13× for a 0.6→0.68m spread), and everything vanishing cleanly on release.

One note: the original's line-gap and ruler-size constants assume Tilt Brush's controller attach points, which sit differently from ours — I retuned those few numbers (line end gaps, ruler width clamp 0.3–0.5m) so the visuals sit right on Quest controllers; the scale math itself is untouched.

[Image #5] I think the colors are quite a mismatch. The ground should blend seemlessly into the geospikes, and the geospikes also seems to have a fog like effect, where the further away ones has lesser opacity? and then the gradient is also much darker. you should take screenshots and use color pickers to compare and fix
[Image: source: /var/folders/xc/nttyc9pj3xngmbjsfbgdbddc0000gn/T/TemporaryItems/NSIRD_screencaptureui_a8buhZ/Screenshot 2026-07-06 at 8.23.40 PM.png]

The reference screenshot makes the mismatch obvious — and I bet the root cause is units again: the environment FBX is authored in Tilt Brush decimeters, so my mountains are 10× too far and the fog density is 10× too weak (0.0025/dm = 0.025/m — which is exactly what creates that near-dark/far-faded look on the peaks). Let me check the skybox gradient colors in the environment asset and sample your screenshot's actual pixels:

input.command: grep -n "Skybox\|m_SkyboxColor" reference/Assets/Resources/Environments/Standard/Standard.asset | head -8; ls "/var/folders/xc/nttyc9pj3xngmbjsfbgdbddc0000gn/T/TemporaryItems/NSIRD_screencaptureui_a8buhZ/" 2>/dev/null

content:
Fixed, with the comparison done by the numbers as you asked. The mismatch had three root causes, all now corrected:

**1. Wrong fog curve.** I was using three.js `FogExp2` (exponential-*squared*), but Tilt Brush environments hardcode Unity's **plain Exponential** mode (`Environment.cs`: "Our Environment assets only use Exponential"). The exported brush shaders' own `ApplyFog` GLSL confirmed it — and revealed the units: density 0.0025 is per *decimeter*, so the effective coefficient is 0.025/m. Three has no plain-exp fog, so the environment materials now patch the fog chunk via `onBeforeCompile` with the exact Unity formula. This single fix produces both effects you pointed at: the far side of the spike ring washes ~90% toward the fog color while nearer ridges stay darker ("lesser opacity further away"), and the ground lightens continuously with distance until it merges into the mountain band — no visible seam.

**2. Wrong geometry scale.** My last edit had shrunk the environment to a 10m ring; the fog math only works out with the ring at its authored ~100–200m depth (that depth spread is what creates the near-dark/far-light ridge layering). Re-exported at meter scale.

**3. Wrong sky gradient source.** I'd used the skybox *tint*; the actual gradient is `m_SkyboxColorA/B` — much darker at the zenith (0.035) with a narrow horizon glow (0.275). Set the dome from those, tuned darker to account for the dome's wide blend band.

**Verified with a pixel-sampling loop** (headless Chrome screenshot → sharp pixel reads at sky-top / sky-mid / horizon / mountain / ground bands, iterated against values sampled from your screenshot): sky top now 15,15,22 vs your 13,13,20; sky mid 18,18,26 vs 18,18,28; mountains sit at fog color and read **lighter than the adjacent sky** — the signature contrast direction from the app. One calibration note: your Quest capture is gamma-dark (headset screenshots skip the sRGB lift), so I matched the structure and relative ordering rather than chasing absolute black in the near ground — in-headset it should read like the app does.

Bonus from the same investigation: brush-stroke fog is now enabled too (`u_fogDensity` = raw 0.0025 — the shaders self-convert), so distant strokes fade toward the horizon exactly like the environment, which is also faithful to the original. 257 tests, typecheck, and build all green.

content:
Fixed — and this time verified by sampling, band by band, against your capture. Two real bugs were making it grey:

**1. Fog was 10× too dense.** I'd "converted" the density from decimeters (0.0025 → 0.025/m), but fog×distance is unit-invariant — the density and the geometry are authored in the *same* units, so no conversion was ever needed. At 0.025/m the exponential fog was tinting even the 3-meter-away floor 10% grey. At the correct 0.0025/m: the ground and near ridges stay essentially untouched (≤1% fog inside 5m), and the wash only builds across the ring's 100→200m depth — black spikes up close, blending into the sky at the far edge, exactly as you described. The brush-stroke fog uniform is set to land at the same 0.0025/m (the exported shaders multiply by 10 internally).

**2. The near floor wasn't black in the first place.** `Geo_FloorForeground`'s authored material albedo is a warm grey (`0x131211`) that only *renders* black in the app under its lighting; I was displaying the albedo raw. Overrode the floors to near-black to match the captured appearance.

Final sampled comparison (mine vs your capture):

| band | mine | yours |
|---|---|---|
| sky top | 15,15,22 | 13,13,20 |
| ground near | 3,3,5 | 4,4,7 |
| ground mid | 4,4,6 | 6,6,9 |
| ridge near | black silhouette | black |
| ridge far | blends into sky | blends into sky |

The side-by-side frames are now structurally identical: black ground with no visible seam into the spike ring, silhouette peaks fading with distance, dark gradient sky. Tests (257) and typecheck stay green. One note for future reference (recorded in memory): the renderer's output transform here is quite flat, so when matching colors against captures, sampling beats reasoning about sRGB curves.

content: No more z-fighting — the platform renders clean from above (that pentagon is the beveled stage in perspective, now 2.5cm proud of the floor). Quick error check after the reload churn, then done:

Fixed. The cause: when I placed the environment pieces in the Blender export, I aligned the stage's **top face** to −0.25m — the exact same plane as `Geo_FloorForeground`. Two coplanar surfaces fighting for the same depth values is textbook z-fighting, and it shimmered worst on the platform because that's where you stand.

In the original layout it can't happen: the stage mesh straddles its pivot by ±2.5cm, and with the prefab node at −0.25m the platform's top surface sits **2.5cm above** the floor plane (it's a raised podium, not an inlay). I re-exported with that authored offset — stage now spans −0.275 to −0.225m — and verified from a top-down view in the emulator: the platform renders as a clean beveled slab with no striping, standing slightly proud of the black floor exactly like the app's.

I think this part can be done using uikit, so that panel would be a hybrid of non-uikit colorpicker + uikit buttons underneath. Also I need you to take a look at the main big panel. that panel has so much stuff on it, its not great. we need to get rid of most stuff on that. I think in the original app there is a wrist menu as well that does a few things so we can potentially bring that in as well, but we need to drastically simplify the entry experience

content:
All three UI changes are in, verified in-headset, with 261 tests and the build green.

**1. Taller panels, full-width brush page.** The root cause of the cramped look: the wand faces were square (0.82×0.82 panel units) while the brush page content is 320×405 — so it aspect-fit at ~79% width, shrinking everything including the header. The prism faces are now 0.82×1.04, which matches the brush page's aspect almost exactly — the 4×4 grid and its header now span the full face width at the same scale as the tools page. The tools page keeps its size, centered on the taller face, and the paint-blocking rectangle became height-aware to match.

**2. content: Swatches are round with spacing now. Next, the ruler — grab with both hands and check the animals:

All three fixed and verified live:

**1. Brush cells staying highlighted.** That's UIKit's hover/active state sticking to an element when the ray leaves mid-press — the same known quirk the tools panel already works around. The brush grid (and the new favorites strip) are UIKit panels but weren't covered by the sweep. `PanelSystem` now runs the same stale-interaction-state clearing for both panels for a few frames after hover ends, so a clicked cell's pressed/hover tint releases properly; the actual *selection* highlight still tracks only the active brush.

**2. Squished ruler.** My UV window width used Open Brush's constant directly, but that constant bakes in *their* quad's aspect ratio. The strip is 4:1 (2048×512), so an unsquished window must satisfy `windowU = (quadWidth / quadHeight) / 4`. The window now derives from the actual quad dimensions (clamped to the strip's edges), so the animals render at true proportions — confirmed in the grab screenshot: dog, human, elephant, giraffe all correctly shaped.

**3. Pill swatches with no gaps.** Root cause: the horizon-kit `<button>` theme injects `paddingX: 16`, which forced each 28px swatch to ≥32px wide — ovals that also swallowed the row's spacing. The swatch and "+" classes now zero out the theme padding explicitly, and the strip got a slightly wider layout budget — the result is true circles with even ~8px gaps, confirmed in the color-face screenshot.

content:
Done — the fix now works exactly as you suggested: **every click clears stale state**, in three layers:

1. **On every click of a brush cell, pager chevron, or favorites swatch/+ button**, the handler immediately sweeps the stuck hover/pressed styling off every other element in that panel (keeping only the element you just clicked). This runs inside our own click handlers, so it's guaranteed regardless of event routing.
2. **A document-level click listener on each wand panel** (tools, brush grid, favorites strip) does the same sweep for any click via event bubbling — this covers the tools buttons without touching their many individual handlers.
3. The existing few-frames-after-hover sweep stays as a fallback for the leave-panel case.

Why it regressed: the old sweep only ran for 3 frames right after the panel lost hover. If a pressed state got stuck while you were off interacting with another panel (release routed elsewhere), and you then came back, the sweep window had long passed — and it never runs *while* you're hovering the panel, so the stale highlight sat there the whole time. Click-driven clearing removes the timing dependency entirely.

The sweep logic itself moved into a shared `uikit-interaction.ts` module (it preserves the clicked element's own feedback by exempting it and its ancestor chain). Typecheck, 261 tests, and the build are green, and the page is reloaded with the fix live if you want to try the repro again.

The A-button mechanism then tunes the anchor itself: hold A → anchor freezes in the scene; release → it re-parents under the grip preserving its world pose, and the resulting grip-local transform (your tuned constant) prints to the console. content:
Baked in. Your tuned values are now the shipped defaults:

- **Right hand**: position `[-0.0224, -0.0382, -0.054]`, quaternion `[-0.4756, 0.0477, -0.2559, 0.8403]` (≈56° pitch down from the grip — consistent with where Quest's ray pose should sit relative to the grip).
- **Left hand**: mirrored across X automatically (negated position X, negated quaternion Y/Z).

Everything that used the ray space now rides these anchors — the draw head, eraser/dropper sphere, camera rig, and the color-wheel ray picking — so the one tuned transform fixes all of them together. The A-button tuning flow stays in place: any time the offset feels off, hold A to freeze the draw head, reposition your hand, release, and send me the new console numbers to bake.

Two things worth knowing:

1. **The desktop emulator will now look offset** — IWER reports grip == ray, so the device-tuned rotation makes the emulator's tip sit pitched down. That's the intended trade (device-first); if it bothers local testing we could branch the default on a user-agent check later.
2. **UIKit panel clicking still uses the engine's target ray internally** (IWSDK's InputSystem) — that's not reachable from app code, so if Quest's ray pose is wrong, panel button aim may still feel offset even though drawing is fixed. If that's noticeable in-headset, the fix would need to go into IWSDK itself (or we patch its ray-space reference at runtime — say the word and I'll look into it).

content:
Both issues addressed:

**Ruler — reworked to the original's actual design.** I went back into the XRRig prefab and the key fact is that Open Brush's ruler quad is *square*, showing only a **quarter of the strip** at a time (`windowU = quadWidth × 0.25` on a square base mesh) — roughly one animal in view. My previous wide-and-short quad forced the window to cover nearly the whole strip, which is why nothing scrolled. Now: 22cm square quad, fixed 0.24 window, panning by `log10(scale) × 0.4277` with edge clamping. Verified live: at scale 1 the window shows exactly the human; after spreading to ~2× it scrolled to the dog. Squirrel and dinosaur await at the extremes (0.1× and 10×).

**Sticky highlight — you were right to push.** To answer your question directly: yes, there *is* another source of grey — the horizon-kit `<button>` component injects its own theme `hover`/`pressed` background fills on every button, independent of our CSS. They key off the same per-element hover/active lists, but my sweeps had two structural holes:
1. **They were per-document** — clicking a tools button only swept the tools doc, never the stale state sitting on the brush doc. Your exact repro (interact elsewhere, come back) was the case they couldn't reach.
2. Nothing cleared state when your ray *returned* to a panel — the stuck tint sat there the whole time you were back on it.

Now: any click on any wand panel sweeps **all three wand documents** (except the element being clicked), and each panel gets swept **the moment it regains hover** — so leftovers are cleared before you can see them, no click required. The few-frames-after-hover sweep stays as the third layer.

All 261 tests, typecheck, and build green. If the grey *still* survives all three sweeps in-headset, the next suspect would be my selection background itself (`rgba(255,255,255,0.28)` on the active brush cell) — but that one is supposed to persist on exactly one cell: the currently selected brush. If you see *two* bright cells with no hover involved, tell me and I'll instrument the selection restyle.

content:
Found it and shipped it. The landing page now opens on **Open Brush's actual intro sketch** — the painted meadow that greets you in the app.

**Where it was:** not a `.tilt` file — the welcome sketch ships as Unity-serialized meshes *embedded inside* `openbrush_intro_simple.prefab` (21MB of YAML; there's a 69MB "full" variant too). No importable model format, so I wrote `scripts/extract-intro-sketch.mjs` which parses the Unity mesh blocks directly — interleaved vertex buffers (position/normal/tangent/color/uv), hex-encoded index buffers — into a compact 7.3MB binary + manifest: 23 nodes, 147k vertices, spanning 17 brushes (Light, Ink, OilPaint, Smoke, Embers, Dots, Streamers, Waveform, VelvetInk…).

**How it renders:** with the *real brush shaders*. Each node's `Intro_*` material maps by name to a brush GUID, and a new `IntroSketchSystem` builds the geometry and requests the corresponding shader material — including particle brushes like Embers and Dots, which work here because the baked meshes carry the original vertex attributes (I added an `allowAnyGeometry` bypass to the shader-eligibility gate, which normally exists only because *our generated* stroke geometry can't feed non-default vertex shaders). So the fireflies wave, the Light strokes glow, everything looks like the app.

Two conversion subtleties that mattered: Unity's left-handed +Z-forward space needed a **Z-mirror with triangle-winding flip** (a 180° rotation would have put it in front but mirrored the lettering), and the sketch is authored *surrounding* the viewer at the origin (±9m), so it's presented in place — you stand in the meadow, exactly like the app's intro.

**Lifecycle matches the original:** it appears on the landing scene and disappears the moment you draw your first stroke (verified live).

One note: the sketch contains **"Powered by Tilt Brush"** lettering — that's authentically what Open Brush ships in this asset (the homage lives on in `openbrush_intro_simple` itself). If you'd rather not show that text, it's isolated in specific nodes and I can filter them at extraction.

All 261 tests, typecheck, and build green.

content:
The checkpoint frames the finished experience. Everything is done — here's the full picture:

## Sketch library (verified live in the emulator, end to end)
Gallery at the wand prism pose (non-rotating) → **New Sketch** clears with a staggered animate-out and fully disposes the gallery → draw → **Save** on the tools panel persists to IndexedDB with a square 256px thumbnail (12.7KB) → **Home** returns to the gallery with the sketch still visible and the new thumbnail in the grid → loading the welcome sketch or a saved sketch animates the old strokes out and the loaded ones in.

## Your "click twice" report — two real bugs, both fixed
1. **Recycled entity index served a stale panel document.** When the gallery was disposed and recreated, `PanelDocument.data.document[entity.index]` could still hold the *previous* panel's document, so click handlers bound to a dead document (hover still worked, clicks didn't). It now gates on `hasComponent(PanelDocument)` and rebinds whenever the document identity changes.
2. **The sticky-highlight sweeps were eating in-flight clicks on all prism panels.** UIKit clicks need the pressed state to survive from pointer-down to pointer-up, but our hover/pressed sweeps could wipe `activeList` mid-press (e.g. ray jitter drops `Hovered` for a frame → 3-frame sweep fires → your release lands on a cleared element). Every sweep now defers while either trigger is held.

Also fixed along the way: the Dots/Smoke/Embers particle shaders failed to compile (they define their own `mat4 inverse()`, illegal once three promotes them to GLSL ES 3.00) — renamed to `tb_inverse` in `prepareBrushShaderSource`, so those intro-sketch strokes actually render now.

## Brushspace rebrand
Welcome panel deleted outright (entity, UIKitML, and ~800 lines of wiring in panel.ts), `forwardHtmlEvents: false` so the 2D view is watch-only, gallery header/page title/.tilt & GLB metadata all say Brushspace. Your original wordmark file was moved to `public/openbrush/intro/brushspace.png` and floats centered above "Powered by Tilt Brush". Root scratch files deleted: `BRUSHSPACE.png`, `brush.pen`, and the two `com.Icosa.OpenBrush-*` captures.

## Git + deploy
Six thematic commits pushed to `git@github.com:felixtrz/brushspace.git` (environment/world-grab, tools/tip-anchor, wand UI, sketch library, rebrand+deploy, journals). The Pages workflow (typecheck → tests → build → deploy) ran green on the first push: **https://felixtrz.github.io/brushspace/** is live — runtime asset fetches now go through a base-aware `assetUrl()` helper so everything resolves under the `/brushspace/` subpath. All 262 tests pass.

I am setting up cloudflare pages for this. any special setup commands? whats the build command? and whats the output directory?

No special setup needed — it's a plain Vite build:

- **Build command:** `npm run build`
- **Output directory:** `dist`
- **Env var (recommended):** `NODE_VERSION` = `22`

Details worth knowing:

- Cloudflare runs `npm ci` automatically from the lockfile before your build command, so nothing extra there.
- The UIKitML panels compile as part of `vite build` (the `compileUIKit` plugin writes `public/ui/*.json` during the build), so the gitignored `public/ui/` is not a problem.
- The vite config uses `base: "./"` and runtime fetches go through the base-aware `assetUrl()` helper, so it works whether it's served at `brushspace.pages.dev` root or any subpath — no config change needed.
- The dev-only plugins (mkcert, the IWER emulator injection) are inert in production builds, and WebXR's HTTPS requirement is covered by Pages by default.
- Largest asset is the intro sketch binary (~7.3MB), well under Cloudflare's 25MB per-file limit.

One heads-up: the GitHub Actions workflow I added deploys to GitHub Pages on every push to `main`. If Cloudflare Pages becomes the canonical host, you may want to delete `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` (or keep both — they don't conflict).

content:
Done — the Enter VR button is in and pushed.

**How it works** (same pattern as blocks): a plain HTML `<button>` in `index.html` (bottom center, white pill, "ENTER VR") whose DOM click carries the user activation WebXR needs, wired in `src/index.ts` to `world.launchXR()`. It subscribes to `world.visibilityState` and hides while a session is active, reappearing on exit. The existing `offer: "always"` native browser UI still works alongside it.

Verified live: button shows on the 2D landing page, disappears inside the session, returns after exit. Pushed as `b9e0d68` — the Pages workflow (and Cloudflare, once connected) will pick it up automatically.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding PeerJS in 2026:

## Current Status

PeerJS Cloud was showing a 200 OK status as of July 6, 2026, indicating the service is operational.

## Cloud Signaling Server Reliability

PeerJS uses a signaling server to broker connections between peers, with the default being the free PeerJS Cloud server. However, 
...[truncated 753 chars]...
ctive and developers are exploring alternatives to the free cloud service.

For production use, the recommendation remains to host your own PeerServer rather than relying on the free cloud version.

content:
Checkpoint captured — the tools panel hosting live with the Share row and code status. The feature is complete, tested, and pushed. Here's the full picture:

## Research → decision

**Transport: PeerJS** (v1.5.5). The join-by-code requirement maps directly onto PeerJS's ID model — the host claims `brushspace-<6-digit-code>` at the free [PeerJS Cloud broker](https://peerjs.com/server/cloud) (currently [operational](https://status.peerjs.com/)) and the guest just dials that ID. No backend, which fits our static hosting; if the free broker ever degrades, a [self-hosted PeerServer on Cloudflare Workers](https://bcnelson.dev/blog/self-hosting-peerjs-cloudflare-workers/) is a one-line config swap. I considered [Trystero](https://github.com/dmotz/trystero) (serverless matchmaking over Nostr/BitTorrent) — clever, but discovery over third-party public relays is less predictable than one broker for an exactly-two-peers session.

**State: custom stroke protocol, not Yjs.** [Yjs](https://github.com/yjs/yjs) is excellent, but our data model makes concurrent edits commute already: strokes are immutable blobs keyed by author-unique GUIDs, and erase/undo/redo all reduce to an idempotent per-stroke visibility boolean — there is nothing for a CRDT to merge, and Yjs's natural transport ([y-webrtc](https://github.com/yjs/y-webrtc)) has [known-dead public signaling servers](https://github.com/yjs/y-webrtc/issues/43). The custom protocol is ~200 lines, fully unit-tested, and adds zero library weight.

## What was built

- **`src/openbrush/collab.ts`** — typed message protocol (hello / snapshot / stroke-progress / stroke-end / stroke-drop / visibility / tip / bye) with validation guards; snapshots stream strokes as individual messages so nothing outgrows the data-channel frame limit. 8 unit tests.
- **`CollabSystem`** — PeerJS lifecycle, single-guest hosting, snapshot on join, live relay, a colored remote-tip presence beacon (~8Hz, canvas-space so world-grab poses stay local), and teardown scoped to the sketch session (Home/New/Load disconnects).
- **StrokeAuthoringSystem** — remote-stroke APIs (upsert/finalize/drop, self-healing whole-stroke progress) and local-op hooks for commit/erase/undo/redo.
- **UI** — Share + Join buttons and a status line on the tools panel; an in-VR numeric keypad for code entry (replaces the prism while open); `?join=CODE` links for 2D browsers.
- **Drop resilience** — abrupt host death takes WebRTC 30s+ to notice via ICE (verified: 90s and still "connected"), so a peer-silence detector treats >10s of quiet as a drop. The survivor keeps every stroke and gets "Host left - the sketch is yours to keep."

## Verified live (host in the emulator, guest in a headless Chrome via CDP)

Join via code through the real PeerJS cloud ✓ · host stroke renders on guest ✓ · guest stroke renders on host ✓ · visibility ops (erase/undo) apply both ways ✓ · guest saved the shared sketch to its own IndexedDB (twice — once *after* the host was SIGKILLed) ✓ · keypad entry ✓ · hosting survives guest departure with the same code ✓. All 269 tests pass; pushed as `72aa660`.

One caveat worth knowing: mid-session the dev tab (after many hot reloads) dropped incoming ops until a fresh reload — production builds never showed this, and a `recentOps` ring in `debugState()` is now there if it ever needs diagnosing.

content:
The new wordmark is in and shipped. Checkpoint captured from inside the session: the painterly BRUSHSPACE headline above "Powered by Tilt Brush", with the gallery on the wand showing the refreshed welcome thumbnail.

What was done:

- **Background removal**: the texture was flattened onto black, so I recovered alpha as the max color channel and un-premultiplied the colors — this keeps the soft dry-brush edges soft instead of producing a hard cutout. The first pass amplified the PNG's near-black compression speckle into low-alpha confetti, so I added a noise floor (alpha < 24 → transparent, soft knee to 48), which cleaned it completely. Processed in a headless Chrome canvas, tight-cropped to 2081×467.
- **Installed** as `public/openbrush/intro/brushspace.png` with the quad aspect updated in IntroSketchSystem (kept your width-50/y-16 tuning).
- **Welcome thumbnail** recaptured with the new wordmark (clean square shot with the Enter VR button and emulator chrome hidden).
- The source file at the repo root was deleted after the texture landed, per your earlier standing instruction.

Pushed as `ac43a89` — the Pages/Cloudflare deploys will pick it up.
